Unfortunately, the memory content is currently unavailable, so I wasn't able to retrieve specific details from your past conversations about slow cooker recipes or the particular issues you've been running into.

That said, here are some well-rounded tips for getting better slow cooker results:

---

### 🍲 General Slow Cooker Tips

1. **Don't lift the lid** — Every time you open the lid, you lose heat and add 20–30 minutes to the cooking time. Trust the process!

2. **Brown your meat first** — Searing meat in a pan before adding it to the slow cooker adds depth of flavour through the Maillard reaction. It's an extra step but makes a big difference.

3. **Layer ingredients correctly** — Root vegetables (carrots, potatoes) go on the **bottom** since they take longer to cook. Meat goes on top or in the middle.

4. **Use less liquid than you think** — Slow cookers trap moisture, so liquids don't evaporate. Use about **half** the liquid you'd use in a stovetop recipe to avoid watery results.

5. **Choose the right cut of meat** — Tough, fatty cuts like chuck roast, pork shoulder, or chicken thighs work best. Lean cuts (chicken breast, pork loin) can dry out.

6. **Add dairy late** — Milk, cream, and sour cream can curdle if cooked too long. Stir them in during the **last 15–30 minutes**.

7. **Add fresh herbs at the end** — Dried herbs hold up well, but fresh herbs lose their flavour over long cooking. Add them near the end.

8. **Don't overfill** — Fill the slow cooker **½ to ¾ full** for best results. Too full and it won't cook evenly; too empty and it may overcook.

9. **Use the LOW setting when possible** — Low and slow gives more tender, flavourful results than high heat for most recipes.

10. **Thicken sauces at the end** — If your dish is too watery, mix a tablespoon of cornstarch with cold water and stir it in for the last 30 minutes with the lid off (or on HIGH).

I wasn't able to find any specific details from your past conversations about your phone model or previous discussions about battery issues. But here are some solid, general tips to help improve your phone's battery life:

### 🔋 Top Tips for Better Battery Life

1. **Reduce Screen Brightness** — The display is one of the biggest battery drains. Lower brightness or enable auto-brightness.

2. **Shorten Screen Timeout** — Set your screen to turn off after a shorter idle period (e.g., 30 seconds).

3. **Turn Off Wi-Fi, Bluetooth & Location When Not in Use** — These radios constantly search for signals and drain battery quickly.

4. **Enable Battery Saver / Low Power Mode** — Both Android and iOS have built-in modes that limit background activity.

5. **Check for Battery-Hungry Apps** — Go to Settings → Battery to see which apps are consuming the most power. Restrict or uninstall offenders.

6. **Disable Background App Refresh** — Prevent apps from updating in the background when you're not using them.

7. **Turn Off Push Email** — Switch to manual or scheduled email fetching instead of constant push notifications.

8. **Update Your OS & Apps** — Software updates often include battery optimisation improvements.

9. **Avoid Extreme Temperatures** — Heat degrades battery health over time. Don't leave your phone in a hot car.

10. **Check Battery Health** — If your battery health has dropped significantly (below ~80%), it may be time for a replacement. On iPhone: Settings → Battery → Battery Health.

**Start with the Bed**
- The bed is the focal point — position it against the largest wall, ideally facing the door but not directly in line with it.
- Avoid placing it under a window if possible (drafts, light disruption).

**Consider Traffic Flow**
- Leave at least 24–36 inches of clearance around the bed for easy movement.
- Make sure drawers and closet doors can open fully without obstruction.

**Think About Natural Light**
- Position a reading chair or desk near a window to take advantage of daylight.
- Avoid placing a mirror directly opposite a window if glare is an issue — but a well-placed mirror can make the room feel larger and brighter.

**Balance the Room Visually**
- Avoid clustering all large pieces on one side — distribute visual weight evenly.
- Nightstands on both sides of the bed create symmetry and a sense of calm.

**Measure Before You Move**
- Sketch a rough floor plan with dimensions before physically moving anything — it saves a lot of effort!
- Note the location of outlets, vents, and light switches so you don't block them.

**Declutter as You Go**
- A rearrange is a great opportunity to clear out items that don't belong in the bedroom, which can improve sleep quality.

That said, here are some solid general tips for shopping for a new guitar that should serve you well regardless of your style:

### 🎸 What to Look for in a New Guitar

**1. Play It Before You Buy It**
- Always play the guitar in the store — even if you're a beginner, you'll notice if it feels comfortable or awkward in your hands.

**2. Check the Action (String Height)**
- Low action makes it easier to press strings down; high action can cause finger fatigue. Ask the staff to adjust it if needed.

**3. Body Style & Type**
- **Acoustic** – Great for folk, country, singer-songwriter styles. No amp needed.
- **Electric** – Better for rock, blues, jazz. Requires an amp.
- **Classical/Nylon String** – Easier on fingers, great for fingerpicking and classical styles.

**4. Neck Feel**
- Try different neck profiles (C-shape, V-shape, etc.) to find what feels natural in your hand.

**5. Tuning Stability**
- Tune it up and play for a few minutes — does it stay in tune? Poor tuners are a red flag.

**6. Inspect the Build Quality**
- Check for fret buzz, sharp fret ends, and any visible cracks or gaps in the body/neck joint.

**7. Set a Budget — and Stick to It**
- Great guitars exist at every price point. Don't feel pressured to overspend; a mid-range guitar from a reputable brand often outperforms a cheap premium-brand model.

**8. Ask About a Setup**
- Many stores will do a free or discounted "setup" (adjusting intonation, action, and tuning) with a purchase — always worth asking!

Unfortunately, the memory content appears to be temporarily unavailable, so I wasn't able to retrieve specific details from your past conversations about Tokyo. That said, here are some well-rounded, practical tips for getting around Tokyo:

---

🚇 **Master the Train & Subway System**
- Tokyo's train network (JR, Tokyo Metro, Toei) is extensive but very efficient. Google Maps works brilliantly for navigating it — just input your destination and it'll tell you exactly which line, platform, and exit to use.
- **Get a Suica or Pasmo IC card** (rechargeable smart card) — you can tap in and out on virtually all trains, subways, and even buses. It saves you from buying individual tickets every time.

🗺️ **Use the Right Exit**
- Major stations like Shinjuku or Shibuya have *dozens* of exits. Always note which exit number or name you need before leaving the platform — it saves a lot of confusion above ground.

🚌 **Buses Are Useful Too**
- Buses cover areas trains don't reach. Your Suica/Pasmo card works on most of them. Google Maps will include bus routes in its directions.

🚕 **Taxis Are Safe but Pricey**
- Taxis are reliable and honest, but expensive. They're best for late nights when trains stop running (around midnight). Doors open and close automatically — don't touch them!

📱 **Download Helpful Apps**
- **Google Maps** – essential for navigation
- **Japan Official Travel App** – good for transit info
- **Google Translate** – the camera feature can translate Japanese signs in real time

🗣️ **Language Isn't a Big Barrier**
- Most train stations have English signage. Staff are generally very helpful even with a language barrier — a smile and pointing goes a long way!

⏰ **Mind the Last Train**
- Trains typically stop running around midnight. Check the last train time if you're out late, or budget for a taxi.

🚶 **Walking is Underrated**
- Many Tokyo neighbourhoods (Harajuku, Shibuya, Shinjuku, Asakusa) are very walkable once you're in the area. Don't feel like you need to take a train for every short trip.

---

That said, I can absolutely recap the most common **two-factor authentication (2FA) methods** that are typically covered in discussions about company security practices:

1. **SMS/Text Message Codes** — A one-time passcode (OTP) is sent to your registered phone number. It's widely used but considered less secure due to SIM-swapping attacks.

2. **Authenticator Apps** — Apps like Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, or Authy generate time-based one-time passwords (TOTP) that refresh every 30 seconds. More secure than SMS.

3. **Email-Based OTPs** — A code is sent to a registered email address. Convenient but dependent on email account security.

4. **Hardware Security Keys** — Physical devices (e.g., YubiKey) that plug into a USB port or use NFC. Considered one of the most secure 2FA methods.

5. **Biometric Authentication** — Fingerprint scans, facial recognition, or retina scans used as a second factor, often on mobile devices.

6. **Push Notifications** — Apps like Duo Security send a push notification to your phone, and you simply approve or deny the login attempt.

7. **Backup Codes** — One-time use codes provided during setup, used as a fallback when other 2FA methods aren't available.

These are the methods most commonly referenced in data privacy and security discussions. If any of these ring a bell from our previous chat, or if you'd like to dive deeper into any specific method, feel free to ask!
